tasks.title: Harden WarnCountingSubscriber follow-ups: release-mode gate, wrapper dedup, comment sync
tasks.description:
Four non-blocking suggestions from the comprehensive review of task 972 (commits 47d21a1b3..1930d5c6d on main). All in `crates/reify-test-support/src/tracing_support.rs`.

**S1: Release-mode test failure (robustness)**
`event_panics_on_non_warn_when_dispatcher_contract_violated` (around lines 568-614) uses `debug_assert_eq!` which is a no-op in release builds. Running `cargo test --release` will produce no panic and the `#[should_panic(expected = "enabled() contract violated")]` attribute will fail. Standard `cargo test --workspace` in `orchestrator.yaml` is debug mode so CI is green today, but the test silently breaks the assumption that release-mode testing passes.

Fix: gate the test on debug builds, e.g. `#[cfg_attr(not(debug_assertions), ignore)]` above the `#[test]` attribute (or `#[cfg(debug_assertions)]` on the `fn`). Document the rationale inline.

**S2: Release-mode robustness asymmetry with `CountingSubscriber`**
Removing the explicit `if level == WARN` guard in `WarnCountingSubscriber::event()` means that in release builds the `debug_assert_eq!` is compiled out and any non-WARN event reaching `event()` will be silently miscounted as a WARN. The contract is currently honored by `enabled()`, but the defensive check was free insurance against future wrapper-code or `enabled()` refactors. `CountingSubscriber::event()` still defensively filters via `counters.get`, creating an asymmetry.

Options:
- (a) Keep a cheap guard alongside the `debug_assert_eq!` (mirrors `CountingSubscriber`)
- (b) Use `assert_eq!` instead of `debug_assert_eq!` so the contract is enforced in all profiles
- (c) Add a brief inline comment explaining why the trust-the-dispatcher asymmetry with `CountingSubscriber::event()` is acceptable

Pick one; the inline-comment approach (c) is cheapest and preserves the silent-defaults alignment the task 972 description called for.

**S3: Subscriber-wrapper duplication**
The test file now has three nearly-identical `tracing::Subscriber` wrapper structs:
- `EventDispatchCounter` in `error_events_rejected_by_enabled_filter` (lines ~230-266)
- A second `EventDispatchCounter` in `counting_subscriber_enabled_rejects_unregistered_levels` (lines ~488-534)
- The new `ForcedEventDispatcher` (lines ~571-606)

Each forwards all trait methods to an inner subscriber and differs only in `enabled()` / `event()`. Substantial boilerplate obscures per-test intent.

Fix: hoist a single generic `ForwardingSubscriber<S>` at module scope with closure or generic parameters for the `enabled`/`event` overrides, or at minimum extract a shared `EventDispatchCounter` struct used by both passing tests. Test-only code — purely maintainability.

**S4: Doc-comment / assertion message drift**
The doc comment for `event_panics_on_non_warn_when_dispatcher_contract_violated` states the assertion message contains `"enabled() contract violated"`, but the actual message in `event()` is `"event() reached with non-WARN — enabled() contract violated"`. The substring match used by `#[should_panic(expected = ...)]` works today but future renames would miss the doc reference.

Fix: either reference the full panic message in the doc comment, or extract the marker string `"enabled() contract violated"` into a `const` and use it in both the `debug_assert_eq!` message and the `#[should_panic(expected = ...)]` attribute (if Rust attribute syntax permits).

tasks.title: Fix let-binding OptionNone typing for let_none_with_typed_annotation
tasks.description: Re-type OptionNone values at let-binding sites so `let x: Option[T] = none` carries Type::Option(T) instead of bare OptionNone. Un-ignores qualified_access_compile_tests.rs::let_none_with_typed_annotation_gets_correct_type.
tasks.details:
## Primary fix (required, ~15 lines)

In `compile_entity`'s `MemberDecl::Let` handling: after compiling the let's value expression, check whether the resulting kind is `OptionNone` and whether `let_decl.type_expr` resolves to `Type::Option(_)`. If both hold, re-create the `OptionNone` value with the resolved `cell_type` so downstream type queries (and goto-def / qualified access) see the annotated Option type rather than the bare sentinel.

- Likely location: at or near the existing let-decl `cell_type` derivation inside `compile_entity`'s `MemberDecl::Let` arm.
- Un-ignore the test: `crates/*/tests/qualified_access_compile_tests.rs::let_none_with_typed_annotation_gets_correct_type` (currently the only remaining `#[ignore]` in that file).
- Parallel logic already exists on the param-default path — mirror its shape rather than inventing a new helper unless both sites deduplicate cleanly.

## Speculative follow-ups (DO NOT fix unless a test surfaces the gap)

Two other param-default sites were identified as not covered by the original param-default fix:
1. `compile_entity`'s nested `port_members` loop.
2. `compile_guarded_members`.

No currently-failing test demands these. Only address them if a regression or new test exposes the gap — otherwise leave alone to avoid speculative change.

## Acceptance

- `let_none_with_typed_annotation_gets_correct_type` passes with `#[ignore]` removed.
- No other tests regress.
- Change is ~15 lines, scoped to the let-binding arm; no drive-by refactoring of the param-default path.

## Memory hints

- Search: "OptionNone typed annotation let binding" in project reify
- Prior context: task-162 restored some/none compilation interception (commit 81d333299)
- Related file: `crates/*/tests/qualified_access_compile_tests.rs`
- Related symbol: `compile_entity` `MemberDecl::Let`

tasks.title: e2e_meta.rs test-quality fixes: sub-structure assertion, redundant tests, brittle indexing
tasks.description:
Post-merge review of task 213 tests in crates/reify-eval/tests/e2e_meta.rs surfaced several test-quality issues that should be addressed:

1. **`e2e_meta_sub_structure_child_meta` (lines 320-365) has a claim/assertion mismatch.** The docstring claims it "verifies that a child template's meta_map entry is built correctly when the entity is referenced as a sub-component in another structure," but the only assertion is on `ValueCellId::new("Part", "label")` — i.e., Part's own top-level template. Nothing is asserted about Assembly, its `sub part = Part()` wiring, or any cell that would differ depending on the sub-component path. Deleting the entire `structure def Assembly { ... }` block would leave the test still passing. Fix: either (a) add an assertion that exercises the sub-component path (e.g., assert Assembly compiles without error AND that some cell keyed off the sub-component is present, or that both templates are in `compiled.templates` with correct `template_registry` wiring), or (b) drop the Assembly definition and rename the test to reflect that it just checks Part's own meta resolution.

2. **`e2e_meta_nonexistent_key_error` (lines 239-270) and `e2e_meta_no_meta_block_error` (lines 279-309) duplicate compiler tests.** They are functionally identical to `meta_access_nonexistent_key_error` (crates/reify-compiler/tests/meta_compile_tests.rs:108) and `meta_access_no_meta_block_error` (same file, line 136). They contain no `engine.eval`/`engine.check` step — the assertion is only about compile-time diagnostics. Fix: either delete these two E2E tests, or make them true E2E by additionally asserting `engine.eval(&compiled)` on the errored module does not panic. Option (b) would give them distinct value vs the compiler-side tests. NOTE: Task 213 requires >=8 tests covering 7 scenarios; if these are deleted, ensure the total count still meets the bar (pre-existing `e2e_meta_access_let_binding` covers the "structure access" scenario).

3. **`e2e_meta_access_on_structure_resolves` (lines 181-229) duplicates `e2e_meta_access_let_binding` (line 15)** almost exactly. The only incremental coverage is `assert_eq!(compiled.templates[0].entity_kind, EntityKind::Structure)`. Fix: add the `entity_kind == Structure` sanity assertion to the existing `e2e_meta_access_let_binding` and drop `e2e_meta_access_on_structure_resolves`. NOTE: verify `>=8 tests` requirement still holds afterwards.

4. **Brittle `compiled.templates[0]` indexing** (lines 152-157, 210-215). Assumes a deterministic ordering of compiled templates. For single-template tests it happens to work, but is fragile and couples tests to compiler output order. Fix: use `compiled.templates.iter().find(|t| t.name == "Welding").unwrap()` / `"Bracket"` etc. instead of `compiled.templates[0]`. This pattern should be applied to any future template lookups too.

Coordination note: Task 1043 (e2e_meta.rs DRY cleanup) will touch the same file. These two tasks should be sequenced (do 1043 first, then this one, or merge them) to avoid churn.

Source: review suggestions from esc-213-4, reviewer_comprehensive.

tasks.title: Consolidate Symmetric URI Normalization Tests into Parameterized Test
tasks.description: Refactor two symmetric URI normalization tests into a single parameterized test using `it.each` in `gotoDefinition.test.ts`.
tasks.details: The task involves consolidating two nearly identical tests in `gotoDefinition.test.ts` into a single `it.each` parameterized test. These tests verify the symmetry of URI normalization using `isSameFile` from `pathUtils.ts`. The refactor should eliminate code duplication and make the symmetry explicit. Use `it.each` with test cases `[['/project/src/foo.ri', 'file:///project/src/foo.ri'], ['file:///project/src/foo.ri', '/project/src/foo.ri']]` to cover both directions. Ensure the tests utilize existing mock factories `makeMockView` and `makeMouseEvent` for consistency and mock fidelity. Populate mock documents realistically to exercise line and character bounds checks, ensuring guards in `gotoDefinition.ts` are tested with real comparisons. Adjust the mock line function to include `to: (n - 1) * 20 + 15` for accurate character bounds. Assert the exact expected anchor to catch off-by-one errors, using `doc.line(8).from + 4 = 144` for the expected anchor. Coordinate with Task 1037 to avoid conflicts and ensure the tightened assertion is maintained.
tasks.testStrategy: Run `npm test -- src/__tests__/gotoDefinition.test.ts` to verify both parameterized cases pass. Ensure `onNavigate` is called zero times and `view.dispatch` is called once per case. Confirm the consolidated test maintains the same behavior as the original tests, with correct mock fidelity and assertions.

tasks.title: lsp_bridge_tests: hoist bridge, pin initialize-error prefix, add wrong-shape params coverage
lib_portable.sh: robustness hardening for portable_timeout POSIX fallback
tasks.description:
Three robustness improvements to portable_timeout() in scripts/lib_portable.sh (POSIX fallback path, lines 89-109):

## S1 — Preserve caller's monitor-mode (`set -m`) state
The `set -m` / `set +m` toggle at lines 91/98 and 102/108 is not state-preserving. If the caller (or a sourcing parent) already had job control enabled, `set +m` will unconditionally disable it as a silent side effect. Save the previous state before toggling and only restore if it was previously unset:
```bash
local _pt_had_monitor=0
case $- in *m*) _pt_had_monitor=1 ;; esac
set -m 2>/dev/null || true
( sleep ... ) &
[ "$_pt_had_monitor" -eq 0 ] && set +m 2>/dev/null || true
```

## S3 — Close SIGKILL PID-reuse race window
Lines 95-96 and 105-106 run `kill -0 "$cmd_pid"` + `kill -9` after a 2s grace, but by that point the main shell has already `wait`ed on `cmd_pid` and the kernel may have reaped it. On a busy system (especially CI containers with aggressive PID recycling) a second process could inherit `cmd_pid` and get killed by mistake. The 2s window is small but non-zero.

Preferred fix (reviewer option a): remove the `kill -0` + `kill -9` escalation from inside the timer subshell entirely, and rely on the main shell's `kill -- -$timer_pid` cleanup path (line 115) to terminate the still-running command+timer as a process group. In the happy path the timer only escalates if the wait window closed with the command still running, which is exactly the timeout case.

## S4 — Document or derive the SIGKILL grace period
The `sleep 2` grace period is a hardcoded magic constant. For callers using very short timeouts (`portable_timeout 1 ...`) it doubles the effective wait. Either:
- Add a comment explaining the 2s choice, OR
- Derive from the timeout duration: `grace=$(( seconds < 10 ? 1 : 2 ))`, OR
- Document in the portable_timeout header comment that the function guarantees termination within `seconds + ~2s`.

## Verification
Run `tests/infra/test_portable_timeout.sh` and `scripts/test_tree_sitter_generate.sh`. Existing tests should still pass.

Source: review escalation esc-994-69 (suggestions S1, S3, S4).

tasks.title: tree-sitter-generate.sh: clean up partial outputs on non-timeout failures + tighten cleanup test pattern
tasks.description:
## S2 — Partial-output cleanup must cover non-timeout failures
In `scripts/tree-sitter-generate.sh` (lines 146-154), the `rm -f src/parser.c src/grammar.json src/node-types.json` cleanup runs only on the timeout path. A non-timeout failure — e.g., `tree-sitter generate` exits non-zero due to a grammar error after having written a partial parser.c — hits the `elif [ "$GEN_EXIT" -ne 0 ]` branch and leaves the corrupted files on disk. Subsequent runs then see them and the staleness check at lines 56-61 treats them as present. This is the same failure mode the diff was trying to prevent; only the timeout path is closed.

Fix: hoist the cleanup into a shared helper or duplicate it on the non-timeout failure branch:
```bash
elif [ "$GEN_EXIT" -ne 0 ]; then
    echo "ERROR: tree-sitter generate failed (exit code $GEN_EXIT)" >&2
    rm -f src/parser.c src/grammar.json src/node-types.json
    exit 1
fi
```

Recommended: define a `_cleanup_partial_outputs()` local function at the top of the generation block and call it on both branches — avoids drift.

## S6 — Tighten Test 16 cleanup assertion
In `scripts/test_tree_sitter_generate.sh:231-232`, Test 16 uses `grep -qE 'rm[[:space:]].*parser\.c'` which is very loose — it would match `# TODO: maybe rm src/parser.c later` or any `rm …parser.c` anywhere in the file (including a newly added non-timeout branch, which is fine, but also unrelated rms). Tighten to require the three output files together:
```bash
grep -qE 'rm -f .*parser\.c .*grammar\.json .*node-types\.json' "$GENERATE_SCRIPT"
```
And ideally add a second assertion that the cleanup appears inside both error branches. With S2's fix, the test should verify cleanup exists in *both* the timeout and non-timeout failure paths.

## Verification
Run `scripts/test_tree_sitter_generate.sh` — existing tests pass, Test 16 tightened. Add a new test case that forces `tree-sitter generate` to fail (e.g., stub it) and asserts the partial outputs are removed.

Source: review escalation esc-994-69 (suggestions S2, S6).

tasks.title: portable_timeout tests: replace vacuous-truth Test 16 and add behavioral SIGKILL escalation test
tasks.description:
## S5 — Test 16 orphan-sleep test is susceptible to vacuous passes
`tests/infra/test_portable_timeout.sh:290-324` runs `portable_timeout 31337 true`. `true` exits almost immediately, and on a fast system the timer subshell may not have forked `sleep 31337` yet by the time the main shell issues `kill -- -$timer_pid`. In that case no orphan could have existed regardless of whether the fix is correct, and the test passes even for a buggy implementation. It does not positively verify the sleep was ever spawned.

Fix (pick one):
- **(a)** Introduce a short delay so the timer subshell is guaranteed to have spawned its inner sleep — e.g., `portable_timeout 31337 bash -c 'sleep 0.2'` combined with a pre-kill `ps` check that the sentinel sleep actually exists briefly.
- **(b)** Add a positive assertion after invocation but before cleanup that the sleep did exist at some point.

Without one of these, the test cannot distinguish the fix from the bug in fast paths.

## S7 — Replace pure-structural Tests 15 and 17 with behavioral SIGKILL escalation test
Tests 15 (lines 273-274) and 17 (lines 333-334) are pure `grep` structural assertions against the source: they verify the literal strings `kill -- -$timer_pid` and `kill -9`/`kill -KILL` exist, but don't verify they work or that the SIGKILL path actually runs on a stuck process.

Add a behavioral test: spawn a command that ignores SIGTERM and assert the process is gone after `portable_timeout` returns within the grace window. Example:
```bash
portable_timeout 1 bash -c 'trap "" TERM; sleep 60' || rc=$?
# Assert rc == 124 AND no `sleep 60` process still exists.
```

Keep the structural tests as cheap guards but complement them with a real behavioral assertion so refactors of the kill path remain protected.

Note: this interacts with the robustness-hardening task (S3) — if the SIGKILL escalation moves out of the timer subshell and into the main-shell cleanup path, the behavioral test still applies but the assertion changes shape (the main shell becomes responsible for killing the stuck child).

## Verification
Run `tests/infra/test_portable_timeout.sh` — existing tests continue to pass; new behavioral test fails against an intentionally-broken SIGKILL path.

Source: review escalation esc-994-69 (suggestions S5, S7).

tasks.title: tree-sitter-generate.sh: clarify MAX_LOCK_ATTEMPTS naming or improve lock-failure error message
tasks.description:
## S8 — Naming clarity and error actionability
In `scripts/tree-sitter-generate.sh`, the two constants at lines 20-24 (`MAX_WAIT_SECS=120`, `MAX_LOCK_ATTEMPTS=75`) are clearly commented, but:

1. The error message at line 110 — `could not acquire generation lock after $MAX_LOCK_ATTEMPTS attempts` — reports iteration count rather than wall time, which is less actionable for an operator trying to understand how long the script waited.

2. `MAX_LOCK_ATTEMPTS` silently depends on the `sleep 1` at the bottom of the poll loop (line 113). If someone ever changes that sleep to `sleep 0.5`, `MAX_LOCK_ATTEMPTS`'s meaning shifts from 75s to 37.5s wall time, undetected.

Pick one:
- **(a)** Rename to `MAX_LOCK_WAIT_SECS=75`, and keep the 1s `sleep` so the iteration count still equals wall-time seconds. Update the error message to `"could not acquire generation lock after ${MAX_LOCK_WAIT_SECS}s"`. Most future-proof.
- **(b)** Keep `MAX_LOCK_ATTEMPTS` but print approximate wall time in the error: `"could not acquire generation lock after ~${MAX_LOCK_ATTEMPTS}s"` and document the 1s-sleep dependency in a comment above the constant.

Low priority — this is a readability/UX improvement, not a correctness bug.

## Verification
Run `scripts/test_tree_sitter_generate.sh`. Tests 9 and 15 reference `MAX_LOCK_ATTEMPTS` by name — if renamed, update those assertions accordingly.

Source: review escalation esc-994-69 (suggestion S8).

tasks.title: Resolve Content Hash Collision Between Value::Point and Value::Matrix
tasks.description: Fix the content hash tag-byte collision between Value::Point and Value::Matrix in reify-types.
tasks.details: To resolve the collision, change the tag byte for `Value::Matrix` from `[18]` to a unique value, such as `[25]`. Audit all `ContentHash::of(&[N])` calls in `content_hash()` to ensure no other collisions exist. Update the inline comment on the Matrix branch to reflect the new tag. Implement a regression test `content_hash_tags_are_unique_across_variants` in the test module of `crates/reify-types/src/value.rs`. This test should construct instances of each `Value` variant, collect their `content_hash()` values, and assert their uniqueness using a `HashSet`. Additionally, check for persistence implications by searching for `content_hash()` usage in caching or storage contexts. If persistence is affected, coordinate a cache-busting release or consider changing the Point tag instead, based on which variant is less critical in storage.

Verify that the regression test correctly asserts the uniqueness of content hashes across variants. Use `rg 'content_hash\(\)' crates/ --type rust | grep -iE 'cache|store|persist|db|disk'` to identify any persistence-related code that may be affected by the tag change.

tasks.title: Add NaN/non-integer guards to LSP position validation and harden cross-file tests
tasks.description:
Follow-up from Task 1054 review triage (esc-1054-65, suggestions S1, S2, S3).

The `isValidLspPosition` helper and the cross-file branch in `gotoDefinition.ts` guard against negative and out-of-bounds values but not NaN or non-integer inputs. Since any comparison with NaN returns false, `NaN` bypasses both `line < 0` and `line + 1 > doc.lines`, reaching `doc.line(NaN + 1)` which throws RangeError (caught by `.catch()` — silent failure). In the cross-file branch, `NaN < 0` is false, so NaN values are forwarded to `onNavigate` and ultimately compute `targetLine.from + NaN = NaN` cursor positions in Editor.tsx.

Three changes:

1. **Add `Number.isInteger` gate** — In `isValidLspPosition`, add `if (!Number.isInteger(line) || !Number.isInteger(character)) return null;` as the first check. This rejects NaN, Infinity, fractional numbers, and non-number types.

2. **Extract shared `isValidPositionShape` helper** — Factor the minimal shape check (`Number.isInteger(x) && x >= 0`) into a helper callable from both the same-file branch (via `isValidLspPosition`) and the cross-file branch (before `onNavigate` call). This centralizes the "what counts as a malformed position" definition per the task 1054 consolidation intent.

3. **Harden cross-file tests** — The two cross-file malformed-response tests (lines 211-264) use a benign `doc.line()` mock, so they pass even if the guard is moved after `doc.line()`. Apply the throwing-`doc.line()` pattern (already used in the line-bounds guard test at line 383-418) or spy on `doc.line` and assert it was never called, to make the tests regression-proof.

Add NaN test cases for both branches. Verify with `npm test -- src/__tests__/gotoDefinition.test.ts`.

memory_hints:
- search: "isValidLspPosition gotoDefinition validation"
- search: "Task 1054 consolidate boundary checks"
- entity: "gotoDefinition.ts"

tasks.title: Harden byte_offset_to_line_col: debug_assert, multibyte tests, fix tautological test
tasks.description:
Improve byte_offset_to_line_col robustness and test coverage (from Task 840 review S1/S2/S5):

1. **debug_assert for offset bounds (S5):** Add `debug_assert!(offset <= source.len())` at the top of `byte_offset_to_line_col` in engine.rs:792. Release builds keep silent clamping; debug/test builds catch stale-span bugs loudly.

2. **Multibyte UTF-8 test (S2):** Add `byte_offset_to_line_col_multibyte_chars` test verifying multi-byte codepoints (e.g. `"αβ\nγ"` where α/β are 2 bytes each). Columns count codepoints, not bytes — this must be verified.

3. **EOF boundary test (S2):** Add `byte_offset_to_line_col_at_source_len` test for offset exactly at `source.len()` — the realistic upper bound for diagnostic spans. Current `offset_beyond_len` test jumps to 100, skipping the boundary.

4. **Fix tautological empty-span test (S1):** `byte_offset_to_line_col_empty_span_identical_coords` (engine_tests.rs:1216-1231) calls a pure function twice with identical args — guaranteed identical by the type system. Either fold the `(2, 1)` assertion into `byte_offset_to_line_col_basic_conversion` as another case, or rewrite at the diagnostic layer: inject a warning with `SourceSpan::new(offset, offset)` and assert `line == end_line && column == end_column`.

tasks.title: Write failing tests + fix OptionNone typing for port members and guarded members
tasks.description: Extend the OptionNone-at-binding-site fixup (currently applied only to top-level entity params, and via task 1083 to top-level entity lets) to cover port-scoped and guarded-group-scoped param/let bindings. Test-first: write failing tests before touching the compiler.
tasks.details:
## Background

A bare `none` literal is compiled with a fallback `result_type = Option[Real]` at `crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs:2161-2164`, because the parser has no contextual type information. Binding sites with an explicit annotation (`param x: Option[T] = none` / `let x: Option[T] = none`) must re-stamp the `OptionNone`'s `result_type` to match, or downstream type queries, goto-def, and qualified access see the wrong inner type. The port-let case is especially bad because the wrong type is also propagated into the scope entry at `lib.rs:5505`, poisoning every later name resolution in that scope.

The reference fix for top-level entity params lives at `crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs:5176-5186`. Task 1083 extends it to top-level entity lets. This task covers the remaining four arms.

## Four sites to cover

All in `crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs`:

1. **Port member param arm** (~`lib.rs:5476-5478`): `compile_expr(...)` with no OptionNone fixup. A port-scoped `param x: Option[Vec2] = none` would stamp the default with `Option[Real]`.
2. **Port member let arm** (~`lib.rs:5491-5520`): `cell_type` is derived from `compiled_expr.result_type` at `lib.rs:5500` with no annotation check. The wrong type also enters the scope at `lib.rs:5505`.
3. **Guarded member param arm** in `compile_guarded_members` (~`lib.rs:6875-6886`): `compile_expr_guarded(...)` with no fixup.
4. **Guarded member let arm** (~`lib.rs:6898-6930`): `cell_type = compiled_expr.result_type.clone()` at `lib.rs:6914` with no annotation check.

## Approach (test-first)

**Step 1 — Write failing tests.** Add one test per site (4 tests total) to the appropriate test file (likely `crates/reify-compiler/tests/qualified_access_compile_tests.rs` alongside the task-1083 test, or `option_compile_tests.rs`). Each test should:
- Construct source exercising the site (e.g., `entity E { port p: Bar { param x: Option[Vec2] = none } }`).
- Compile it.
- Assert the resulting `ValueCellDecl`'s `cell_type` (and where applicable, the `default_expr`'s `result_type`) matches the annotated `Option[T]` — NOT the fallback `Option[Real]`.

**Step 1b — Grammar reachability check.** If any of the four constructs is not admitted by the current grammar (`tree-sitter-reify`), mark that site unreachable, close out its sub-scope, and document it in the PR. Do not write compiler logic for an unreachable path.

**Step 2 — Fix the reachable sites.** Mirror the `lib.rs:5181-5184` pattern:
```rust
if matches!(&compiled.kind, CompiledExprKind::OptionNone)
    && matches!(&cell_type, Type::Option(_))
{
    compiled = CompiledExpr::option_none(cell_type.clone());
}
```
For the let arms, the fixup needs to happen *before* `cell_type` is derived from `compiled_expr.result_type` — resolve the annotation from `let_decl.type_expr` first (mirroring the pattern task 1083 establishes for the top-level entity let arm), then fix up.

**Step 2b — Consider extraction.** If the four fixups look identical, extract a `fixup_option_none(compiled, declared_type) -> CompiledExpr` helper. Do not extract if sites diverge; three similar call sites is fine.

## Dependencies

- Task 1083 (top-level entity let fix) establishes the annotation-resolution pattern for let arms. This task should land *after* 1083 so the let-arm pattern has a reference implementation.

## Acceptance

- 4 new tests (or fewer if some sites are unreachable), each failing on main before the fix and passing after.
- Fix mirrors task 1083 / `lib.rs:5181-5184` without introducing new behavior.
- No drive-by refactoring of unrelated code.
- `cargo test -p reify-compiler` clean, no clippy warnings in the touched file.

## Memory hints

- Search: "OptionNone typed annotation let binding"
- Related task: 1083 (top-level entity let fix)
- Related files: `crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs`, `crates/reify-compiler/tests/qualified_access_compile_tests.rs`, `crates/reify-types/src/expr.rs`
- Prior context: task 162 restored some/none compilation interception (commit 81d333299)
- Feedback: `feedback_silent_defaults_pattern` — avoid silent fallback types on error/uncovered paths

tasks.title: Strengthen gradient exact-restore test coverage (follow-up to task 1006)
tasks.description: crates/reify-expr/tests/gradient_tests.rs test-quality fixes from esc-1006-778 review triage. The current tests do not actually guard the exact-restore fix.
tasks.details:
(1) Replace gradient_single_point_param_irrational_coords body: drop the ~47-line manual field reconstruction, call make_3d_dot_product_gradient_field() AND switch the underlying field to a nonlinear function (e.g. dot(p,p) with expected gradient 2p) so the test actually exercises exact-restore — linear dot(p,[1,2,3]) provides zero regression protection because its gradient is constant.
(2) Tighten gradient_single_point_param_quadratic tolerance from 1e-3 to ~1e-9 (or document why a looser tolerance is intentional), since current tolerance is ~13 orders of magnitude above the ~4-ULP arithmetic-vs-exact-restore delta.
(3) While touching these tests, extract an assert_gradient_vector(result, expected, tol, label) helper and apply it to gradient_single_point_param_quadratic, gradient_single_point_param_irrational_coords, and the pre-existing gradient_3d_field_single_point_param (3 sites with verbatim assertion loops).

Source: review triage of esc-1006-778 (19 suggestions, 5 reviewers). Flagged as misleading_test_coverage and tolerance_too_coarse_for_stated_intent by reviewer_test_analyst.

tasks.title: Harden point_scratch take/recover idiom in compute_numerical_gradient_at_point (follow-up to task 1006)
tasks.description: crates/reify-expr/src/lib.rs naming + defensive-assertion + DRY fixes from esc-1006-778 review triage.
tasks.details:
In compute_numerical_gradient_at_point (~line 788-836):
(1) Rename point_scratch to work_point to match the work_ prefix convention used by work_coords and work_args in the same function.
(2) Extract the push / std::mem::take / apply_lambda / if-let-pop recover sequence (duplicated verbatim for the f_plus and f_minus evaluations) into a local closure or private helper.
(3) Inside the helper (or after each recovery site), add debug_assert_eq!(work_point.len(), n) to catch silent empty-vec recovery if apply_lambda's signature ever changes away from &[Value]. This fragile load-bearing invariant was independently flagged by 5 of 6 reviewers.

Source: review triage of esc-1006-778 (19 suggestions). Flagged across reviewer_architect_reviewer, reviewer_comprehensive, reviewer_performance, reviewer_reuse_auditor, reviewer_test_analyst, reviewer_robustness.

tasks.title: Harden find_self_reading_test_fns scanner and cover attribute-gap edge case (follow-up to task 1059)
tasks.description: tree-sitter-reify/tests/build_logic_tests.rs — tighten test_self_read_paths_use_manifest_dir assertions and add one missing scanner edge case.
tasks.details:
From esc-1059-785 review triage (4 suggestions, 1 skipped):

(1) test_self_read_paths_use_manifest_dir is effectively tautological: find_self_reading_test_fns discovers tests whose body contains (THIS_FILE), then the loop asserts each body contains (THIS_FILE) — can never fail by construction. Only the `>= 3` count is a real guard, and it's stale: if a 4th self-reading test is added and one original regresses, the count still passes.
Fix: broaden the scanner's discovery criterion so the loop becomes a genuine cross-property check. For example, scan for functions that contain read_to_string/File::open applied to any path argument, then assert they specifically use (THIS_FILE) rather than a bare relative path. Alternatively, add a complementary negative scan for bare path strings like "tests/build_logic_tests.rs" outside of comments, asserting none are used as function arguments.

(2) Add edge-case coverage to test_find_self_reading_test_fns_discovers_dynamically for a `#[test]` function with an intermediate attribute (e.g. `#[test]\n#[allow(unused)]\nfn test_with_attr() { let _ = read(THIS_FILE); }`). find_cfg_unix_test_fns's test covers this, but the new scanner's test doesn't — the code handles it via the starts_with('#') branch, so the test is straightforward to add.

Skipped: state-machine duplication between find_self_reading_test_fns and find_cfg_unix_test_fns — reviewer self-marked "no action required" unless a third scanner is ever needed.

Source: esc-1059-785.

tasks.title: Strengthen bit-identity test edge cases in value.rs
tasks.description:
In `crates/reify-types/src/value.rs`, the bit-identity test functions for Scalar, Complex, and Orientation have several coverage gaps identified during Task 913 review:

1. **Missing ordering assertion for Complex `im` neg-zero** (~line 3222): The neg-zero-in-`im` sub-case asserts `assert_ne` and antisymmetry but omits `assert!(neg_im < pos_im)`, unlike every other neg-zero sub-case (Scalar, Complex `re`, Orientation `w`). Add this one-liner.

2. **Both-component edge cases for Complex** (~line 3149-3224): NaN and neg-zero are tested in `re` and `im` independently, but not simultaneously. Add a sub-case like `re: f64::NAN, im: -0.0` vs `re: f64::NAN, im: 0.0` to verify lexicographic chaining when the first component compares Equal.

3. **Orientation neg-zero in non-`w` component** (~line 3577-3643): The `nan_z` spot-check covers NaN in a non-w component, but neg-zero is only tested in `w`. Add a spot-check with neg-zero in `z` (or `x`/`y`) where `w` is identical, to exercise the lexicographic fallthrough path.

4. **Distinct NaN bit patterns** (~line 2258-2303): All NaN tests use `f64::NAN` for both values (identical bit patterns). Add a sub-case with two distinct NaN payloads (e.g., `f64::from_bits(0x7ff8000000000001)` vs `f64::NAN`) and verify `assert_ne` for both PartialEq and Ord, strengthening the to_bits() contract.

Memory hints: search "Value bit-identity to_bits PartialEq Ord edge cases"

tasks.title: Extract assert_ord_consistent test helper for bit-identity tests
tasks.description:
In `crates/reify-types/src/value.rs` (lines ~2258-3643), the three bit-identity test functions (`value_scalar_bit_identity_neg_zero_and_nan_consistent`, `value_complex_bit_identity_nan_and_neg_zero_consistent`, `value_orientation_bit_identity_nan_and_neg_zero_consistent`) share an identical structural pattern:

  create pair → assert_eq/ne → check cmp consistency → check antisymmetry → check ordering direction

Extract a small test helper like:
```rust
fn assert_ord_consistent(a: &Value, b: &Value, expect_equal: bool)
```
that takes two Values and an expected equality boolean, then asserts the full PartialEq↔Ord contract (equality match, antisymmetry, and optionally ordering direction). This would:
- Reduce repetition across all three tests
- Make it impossible to accidentally omit a consistency check (as happened with the missing `neg_im < pos_im` assertion)
- Provide a reusable pattern for future Value variant tests

Memory hints: search "Value bit-identity test helper assert_ord_consistent"

tasks.title: portable_timeout: post-review polish (readability, docs, test robustness)
tasks.description:
Five small improvements to portable_timeout code and tests from task 1088 review (esc-1088-777):

## S1 — Fix Test 18 comment tense (test_portable_timeout.sh:343-345)
The comment says "Current code unconditionally runs set +m..." in present tense, but the patch already fixed this. Reword to past tense: "Before this fix, the code unconditionally ran set +m..." to make it a regression-guard comment.

## S2 — Replace `&&`/`||` idiom with explicit `if` (lib_portable.sh:112, 120)
`[ "$_pt_had_monitor" -eq 0 ] && set +m 2>/dev/null || true` relies on left-associative shell operator precedence. Replace with explicit `if [ ... ]; then set +m 2>/dev/null || true; fi` at both lines 112 and 120.

## S3 — Add caller guidance for SIGTERM-only termination (lib_portable.sh:42-49)
The header documents SIGTERM-only but doesn't explain how callers should detect/handle the case where the command ignores SIGTERM. Add a brief note, e.g. "Callers needing SIGKILL can wrap the command or check process liveness after portable_timeout returns."

## S4 — Scope Test 17 grep to function body (test_portable_timeout.sh:335-336)
Test 17 greps the entire file for `kill -9`/`kill -KILL`. Future functions could legitimately use SIGKILL. Scope the grep to the portable_timeout function body using `sed -n '/^portable_timeout()/,/^}/p'`.

## S5 — Add Test 18b for degraded-path monitor-mode preservation (test_portable_timeout.sh)
Test 18 only exercises the normal path (mktemp succeeds). Add a Test 18b using MKTEMP_FAIL_SETUP to verify monitor-mode preservation on the degraded path (mktemp fails, lines 116-120).

## Verification
Run `tests/infra/test_portable_timeout.sh` and `scripts/test_tree_sitter_generate.sh`.

Memory hints: search "portable_timeout POSIX fallback" and "task 1088 robustness hardening"

tasks.title: Unify guard-state hash format across eval() and edit_param()
tasks.description:
The guard-state hash format strings differ between eval() and edit_param() in crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs, causing the same logical guard state to produce different topology fingerprints depending on the code path:

- Site 1 (eval(), ~line 1797): `format!("{:?}", gv)` — hash of just the debug representation
- Site 2 (edit_param(), ~line 2092): `format!("guard:{}={:?}", g.guard_cell, val)` — includes cell ID prefix

This inconsistency could cause subtle bugs if fingerprints are ever compared across eval/edit_param boundaries. Currently the test doesn't cross-assert F1==F3 (same guard state computed via different paths).

## Fix
Unify to the more descriptive `guard:{}={:?}` format in both paths (or choose a single canonical form). Then add a test assertion that F1 == F3 to verify cross-path fingerprint consistency.

## Memory hints
- search: "guard state hash topology fingerprint"
- search: "feedback_silent_defaults_pattern"
- entity: task 1003 (parent task that added the current comments/expect() calls)

tasks.title: Add keyword assertions to task-1045 meta error path tests
tasks.description:
In `crates/reify-eval/tests/e2e_meta.rs`, the two task-1045 regression tests (`e2e_meta_access_missing_key` at ~line 536 and `e2e_meta_access_no_meta_block` at ~line 574) only assert `!errors.is_empty()`. This means any unrelated compile error (e.g., a type mismatch) would satisfy the assertion, masking a regression in the meta-specific error path.

Add a secondary loose keyword assertion to each test checking that at least one error message contains `"meta"`, e.g.:
```rust
assert!(
    errors.iter().any(|d| d.message.contains("meta")),
    "expected a meta-related error, got: {:?}",
    errors
);
```

This keeps the tests wording-resilient (they intentionally avoid exact message checks like the task-213 tests do) while preventing false-positive passes from unrelated errors.

tasks.title: Add descriptive labels to parameterized URI normalization test in gotoDefinition.test.ts
tasks.description: Improve test output readability by adding descriptive labels to the `it.each` parameterized URI normalization symmetry test. Currently the test description renders verbose full URIs in failure output. Use labeled tuples with a descriptive tag instead.

])(

To labeled tuples:

  ['bare-path → file://', '/project/src/foo.ri', 'file:///project/src/foo.ri'],

)

tasks.title: Guard collection subs in self-resolution paths
tasks.description:
The `self.member` branch (lib.rs:2669) and `self.sub.member` branch (lib.rs:2710) check `sub_component_types` without excluding collection subs. For a collection sub like `sub items : List<Bolt>`, `self.items` returns `Type::StructureRef("Bolt")` (single-entity ref) instead of being rejected or producing a collection type, causing silently wrong evaluation results at runtime.

Fix: Add `&& !scope.collection_sub_names.contains(member.as_str())` guard at line 2669 and `&& !scope.collection_sub_names.contains(sub_name.as_str())` guard at line 2710. Optionally emit a diagnostic directing users to indexed access for collection subs.

Also add test coverage for `self.collection_sub` and `self.collection_sub.member` in self_keyword_tests.rs to assert correct behavior.

tasks.title: Add negative test pinning dimensioned-domain gradient assertion skip
tasks.description:
In crates/reify-expr/tests/gradient_tests.rs, add a test that verifies a gradient field with a dimensioned domain (e.g., domain=Scalar[LENGTH]) and a mismatched codomain (e.g., declared Scalar[MASS] but lambda returns Scalar[LENGTH]) does NOT panic, even though the codomain is wrong.

This pins the intentional design decision in lib.rs (~line 854): the debug assertion only fires for dimensionless domains (domain_dim.is_none()). With dimensioned domains, `make_arg` passes Scalar<domain_dim> which causes the lambda to naturally return a dimensioned value regardless of the declared codomain — so the assertion is skipped.

The test should:
- Create a field with domain=Scalar[LENGTH], codomain=Scalar[MASS], lambda |x| 2*x
- Compute gradient, sample at a point
- Assert it returns a value (not Undef) and does NOT panic

tasks.title: Export INIT_PARAMS_ERR_PREFIX from reify-lsp to eliminate test-side duplication
tasks.description:

tasks.title: Fix silent unwrap_or(0.0) in solver uniqueness check
tasks.description:
In `crates/reify-constraints/src/solver.rs:763,767`, the uniqueness verification code uses `.unwrap_or(0.0)` when extracting solved parameter values. If either the original or perturbed solve returns Undef or a non-numeric value for a param, both default to 0.0 — making diff=0.0 and incorrectly reporting the solution as unique.

This violates the "errors must be noisy" convention. A solver that returns Undef/missing values for auto params is a bug that should be detected, not silently absorbed.

Fix: if either value is None (missing) or non-numeric, either (a) return false (non-unique / cannot verify), or (b) skip that param with a tracing::warn!. Do NOT default to 0.0.

tasks.title: Log warning on delta_to_events serialization failure instead of silent skip
tasks.description:
In `gui/src-tauri/src/diff.rs:148-161`, delta_to_events() silently skips any mesh/value/constraint that fails serde_json::to_value(). If serialization fails (e.g., unexpected NaN in a field), the frontend never receives the update event and its state becomes permanently stale for that item — with no indication of failure.

Fix: replace `if let Ok(val) = serde_json::to_value(mesh)` with a match that logs `tracing::warn!("failed to serialize {}: {}", item_id, err)` on the Err branch. Consider also emitting an error event so the frontend can show a diagnostic.

tasks.title: Log error when sidecar tool-result write fails instead of .ok() swallow
tasks.description:
In `gui/src-tauri/src/claude_bridge.rs:292`, the tool result write to the sidecar is swallowed with `.ok()`:
```rust
write_to_sidecar(&mut *writer, &response).await.ok();
```
If this write fails, the sidecar hangs forever waiting for a ToolResult that never arrives. The Claude session becomes stuck with no error reporting.

Fix: replace `.ok()` with error handling that (a) logs `tracing::error!("failed to send tool result to sidecar: {err}")` and (b) emits a `claude-error` event to the frontend so the user sees the failure.

tasks.title: Extract guard_state_fingerprint helper in reify-eval
tasks.description:
Eliminate 3-site duplication of guard fingerprint computation in crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs: eval() lines ~1508-1517, edit_param() Site 1 lines ~1823-1831, edit_param() Site 2 lines ~2111-2125. This format-string drift across sites is exactly what caused the bug task 1112 fixed.

Extract a helper: `fn guard_state_fingerprint(groups: &[GuardedGroup], values: &HashMap<ValueCellId, Value>, strict: bool) -> ContentHash`. Sites 1 and eval() pass strict=false (unwrap_or(Value::Undef)); Site 2 passes strict=true (.expect). Preserve existing comments explaining strict-vs-lenient rationale at the helper level.

tasks.title: Improve guard fingerprint tests: multi-guard + rationale comment
tasks.description:
Two related test improvements in crates/reify-eval/tests/guard_eval.rs from review of task 1112:

1. **Multi-guarded-group test for ID disambiguation.** The Site 1 comment in crates/reify-eval/src/lib.rs (around line 1822) explicitly calls out "two guards holding the same value produce different hashes" as a critical correctness property, but no existing test exercises 2+ guarded groups. Add a regression test: build a template with two guarded groups whose guard cells both evaluate to Bool(true), capture fingerprint F_tt; then edit one guard to false (F_tf) and the other to false (F_ft). Assert F_tt != F_tf, F_tt != F_ft, F_tf != F_ft — proving the fingerprint is sensitive to which specific guard cell holds which value, not just the multiset of guard values. This protects the guard_cell ID disambiguation property across future refactors.

2. **Rationale comment on cross-path test.** The test eval_edit_param_guard_fingerprint_cross_path_consistency (around line 1387) overlaps significantly with the updated edit_param_guard_fingerprint_round_trips (lines 1258-1374), which now asserts F1==F3 cross-path equality at line 1347. Add a brief doc comment explaining why both tests exist: round_trips covers F2==F4 and value assertions; this focused test is a minimal regression reproducer for the cross-path bug fixed in task 1112.

Source: review suggestions on task 1112, escalation esc-1112-816.
